planting.note: Plant nursery-propagated rhizome divisions in fall in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral, well-drained soil in partial to full shade; never collect toadshade from wild populations — purchase only nursery-propagated plants. The stalkless (sessile) flower sits directly atop the three mottled leaves.

directions: Plant nursery-grown divisions in fall in humus-rich, moist, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial to full shade — toadshade trillium is one of the most distinctive of the eastern native trilliums, recognizable immediately by its sessile (stalkless) dark maroon flower that sits directly upon the three large, dramatically mottled leaves without an intermediate stalk. The heavily mottled, silver-and-green leaves are among the most beautiful of any native spring wildflower, providing ornamental interest even before the flowers open. A wide natural range stretches from New York west to Kansas and south to Georgia, growing in rich, moist hardwood forests and along stream banks. Like all trilliums, toadshade takes 7 years to bloom from seed — always purchase nursery-propagated plants and never collect from wild populations. The flowers attract small flies and beetles as pollinators; the seeds are dispersed by ants. An outstanding native wildflower for the shaded woodland garden.

planting.note: Direct sow seed in fall or start indoors in late winter in moist, slightly acidic to neutral, humus-rich, well-drained to average soil in partial to full shade; blue wood aster is one of the most shade-tolerant of all native asters, blooming reliably in light to moderate shade where almost no other asters will flower.

directions: Sow seed in fall or start indoors in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial to full shade — blue wood aster is exceptional among native asters for its genuine shade tolerance, producing masses of small, light blue to lavender ray flowers with yellow centers in September and October in light to moderate shade where most asters cannot bloom. The heart-shaped basal leaves (giving the alternate common name 'heart-leaved aster') are distinctive and attractive through the growing season. It grows naturally in open woodland and woodland edge settings throughout the eastern US, forming loose colonies that self-sow in appropriate conditions. An outstanding source of late-season nectar for migrating monarchs, native bees, and other pollinators that are searching for food in fall. One of the most valuable native wildflowers for the shaded fall garden where late-season color and pollinator support are both needed.

planting.note: Direct sow seed in fall or stratify and sow in early spring in moist, slightly acidic to neutral, humus-rich, well-drained soil in partial to full shade; Jacob's ladder self-sows reliably in appropriate conditions and forms steadily expanding colonies over time.

directions: Sow seed in fall in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial to full shade — Jacob's ladder is a charming native spring wildflower named for its distinctive pinnately compound leaves whose paired leaflets march ladder-like up either side of the central stem, resembling the Biblical Jacob's ladder to heaven. Clusters of nodding, bell-shaped blue-violet flowers appear in April and May on stems 8–16 inches tall, providing valuable early-season nectar for bumblebees and other native bees. It grows naturally in rich, moist, shaded forest and woodland slopes throughout the eastern US, forming loose colonies in appropriate conditions. The foliage remains attractive through summer in cool, moist locations before dying back in late summer. Self-sows reliably and naturalizes gently without becoming invasive. An excellent companion for native ferns, trilliums, and spring ephemerals in the shaded native plant garden.

planting.note: Direct sow seed in early spring or fall in moist to average, slightly acidic to neutral, humus-rich to average soil in full sun to partial shade; short-toothed mountain mint tolerates damp conditions and is one of the most adaptable of the mountain mints to average garden soils.

directions: Sow seed in early spring or fall in moist to average, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade — short-toothed mountain mint is widely regarded as one of the single most valuable native plants for pollinators in the eastern US, producing dense, flat-topped clusters of tiny white to pale pink flowers whose nectar is irresistible to a remarkable diversity of native bees, wasps, beetles, flies, and butterflies. The silvery-white leaf bracts surrounding each flower cluster give the plant a striking frosted appearance at bloom time. Strongly mint-scented throughout, it repels deer and rabbits. Unlike most mountain mints, it tolerates partial shade and moist soils well, making it more adaptable than the narrow-leaved species. Spreads by rhizome to form a colony; divides easily to share. An outstanding native herb for mixed borders, rain gardens, and any setting where maximum pollinator support is the primary goal.

planting.note: Surface sow seed in fall (needs light to germinate; do not cover seed) in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, average to poor soil in full sun to partial shade; prairie alumroot is more drought-tolerant and sun-tolerant than the woodland alumroot species and thrives in the open prairie garden.

directions: Surface sow seed in fall (light required for germination) in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, average to dry soil in full sun to partial shade — prairie alumroot is the native Heuchera of midwestern prairies and prairie openings, producing airy wands of tiny greenish-white flowers on slender stems above mounds of rounded, shallowly lobed, evergreen to semi-evergreen leaves. While the garden coral bells (H. sanguinea) and its many modern hybrids are more familiar in cultivation, prairie alumroot is a tough, drought-tolerant native species offering excellent ecological value — it is an important nectar source for hummingbirds and long-tongued native bees and is the host plant for specialist Heuchera-feeding insects. More drought-tolerant and sun-hardy than the woodland-origin Heuchera species, making it better suited to drier prairie-garden settings. Evergreen rosettes add winter interest.
